risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Two randomisation lists are computer generated by a statistician otherwise not involved in the study." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence prepared by an independent statistician, which meets criteria for truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "Randomisation by a third party not involved in the study using a computerised random-number generator." Comment: Although the computerised random-number generator refers to sequence generation, the explicit use of a third party not involved in the study to perform randomisation indicates the recruiter could not foresee the upcoming assignment, which is an adequate concealment mechanism for this domain.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design takes into account the principles set out in the Helsinki declaration" and "The intervention investigated in PreFalls is a 16 week program with focus on fall prevention"; participants in the intervention arms received a clearly active exercise/CBT-style program, while the control group received usual care and no blinding of participants or personnel is described. Comment: Because the intervention was behavioural/exercise-based and obviously distinguishable from control, participants and those delivering the program would have known allocation. Outcomes included self-reported fear of falling, quality of life, physical activity, and falls-related measures, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of assignment, so performance bias is high.

Summary: The study reports that falls were assessed by patient self-report in a daily fall diary and follow-up telephone interviews, and that other outcomes included patient questionnaires (EuroQol, FES-I, PAR) plus performance tests (TUG, chair stand, balance tests). It does not state that outcome assessors were blinded, and for the self-reported and questionnaire outcomes the participant is effectively the assessor. Because blinding of participants/outcome assessors is not explicitly reported, and the outcomes are a mix of subjective and objective measures, the risk of detection bias cannot be judged confidently.

Quote: "In the 'timed up and go test' ..." and "In case that during follow-up some patients stop to fill in the diary they will be phoned to ask for basic information on falls (number of falls, injuries and consequences)." Also: "Analyses of the primary endpoint ... will be performed on base of the full-analysis set (complete information regarding the primary outcome parameter). Within this population, all subjects are considered regardless to their actual compliance ... (intention to treat principle)." Comment: The paper indicates active follow-up procedures for missing diary data, an ITT/full-analysis-set approach for the primary outcome, and no signal of substantial or clearly imbalanced attrition or exclusions related to outcomes. This is sufficient to judge attrition bias as unlikely.
